FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study explicitly named in title, methods describe recruitment of >10,000 children age 9-10, NIH-funded, 21 U.S. sites, data from NIMH Data Archive (NDA), release 2.0.1

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: machine learning models predicting DRD from baseline ABCD questionnaire and MRI data; elastic net, random forest, gradient boosting, support vector regression approaches; cross-validation and held-out test set

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Experimental and Clinical Psychopharmacology (2022)

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Screen media activity (generic composite) paired with DRD (individual-trait outcome). Abstract states: 'Key predictive variables were neuropsychological test performance at baseline, socioeconomic status, screen media activity, psychopathology, parenting, and personality.' Results section (Table 3, Figure 3) shows 'Weekend Screen Time (parent report)' coefficient -1.66 (p=5.50E-11), 'Weekday Screen Time (youth report)' coefficient -1.14 (p=6.21E-13), 'Weekend Screen Time (parent report)' coefficient -0.98 (p=2.24E-06) as part of elastic net model. DRD is individual-trait-level outcome (behavioral, psychological characteristic linked to cognitive function and reward processing)

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric results reported: elastic net regression coefficients for screen time variables (e.g., -1.66, -1.14, -0.98) with p-values and FDR-corrected p-values; R-squared values for model performance (e.g., R²=4.2% in cross-validation); feature importance metrics

FT_IC4.evidence: Full inferential analysis with quantitative results: machine learning elastic net models with numeric coefficients, p-values, R² metrics, and statistical significance testing reported in Table 3 and throughout results section

FT_EC7_reason: Design is baseline-to-1-year follow-up (longitudinal), but screen media time appears as a PREDICTOR (IV) of DRD outcome (DV), not screen media as DV. Cross-sectional carve-out does not apply; SM is consistently IV-side. FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: Paper meets all inclusion criteria. Screen time measured as both weekday and weekend, youth and parent report; DRD (delayed reward discounting) is behavioral/individual-trait outcome; substantial numeric results throughout with multiple feature importance coefficients and R² values

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study explicitly named as source; methods state 'The participants were from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study, recruited by probability sampling from the USA population'; ages 9.9±0.6 years; N=10,650 children

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional associations between parental psychological problems (measured via Adult Self-Report questionnaire) and children's screen media behaviors (TV, videos, video games, social media, mature-rated media) using logistic/linear regression

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Acta Paediatrica (2022)

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 search: Abstract and results focus on TV watching, video watching, gaming, and social media consumption as DVs, but no phone/SM-specific IV with isolated coefficient found in Step 1. Step 2 composite check: Main analysis examines composite 'screen media' time (TV + videos + video games + social media) but does not isolate phone/SM modality separately. Parental psychological problems (IV) are demographic/family-contextual (not individual-trait-level for the child). Other-side rule violated: parental psychological problems are parental-level variables, and the paper does not pair phone/SM variables with child individual-trait outcomes. Results show parental problems → child screen time/mature media, but this is demographic/parental-contextual predictor → screen behavior, not phone/SM variable ↔ individual-trait child outcome

notes: Paper fails FT-IC2 on two grounds: (1) composite screen time bundles TV + video games + videos without phone/SM-specific isolation; (2) other-side of relationship (parental psychological problems) is parental-level, not child individual-trait-level. Exclusion code: FT-EC2

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD study explicitly stated: 'Data (2016-2020) from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study were analyzed'; ages 12-13 primarily; N=6,749; methods describe ACE score and screen time/physical activity measured in May 2020 during COVID-19 pandemic

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: linear regression analyses estimating associations between ACE score and screen time and physical activity; adjusted models controlling for confounders; cross-sectional analysis of pandemic-period data

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Academic Pediatrics (2022)

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 search: Abstract describes associations between ACE score (IV) and screen time (DV) but does not isolate phone/SM-specific components or report a phone/SM-specific labeled numeric estimate. No mention of 'smartphone use,' 'social media time,' 'texting,' or other phone/SM-specific modality. Step 2 composite check: Screen time measured as composite (daily hours) without internal breakdown of phone vs. TV vs. other devices reported in abstract. Most critically, other-side rule violation: ACEs (Adverse Childhood Experiences) are trauma/family-contextual variables, not individual-trait-level outcomes on the non-SM side. The relationship being tested is ACE score → screen time; screen time is the outcome (DV), but ACE is not an individual-trait outcome; instead, the paper pairs SM/screen time (IV conceptually, though authors frame it as DV) with ACE trauma exposure (contextual/family history, not individual-trait-level child characteristic like behavioral, cognitive, well-being, or personality trait)

notes: Paper fails FT-IC2 on two grounds: (1) Screen time is not isolated to phone/SM modality; composite measure without internal breakdown reported. (2) Other-side (ACE score) is contextual/family-level trauma exposure, not individual-trait-level outcome (ACEs are classified as contextual-only per criteria §2.4). Exclusion code: FT-EC2
